First, I remember that sautéing usually involves cooking with fat, like butter or oil. Sherry is a type of fortified wine, which is more of a cooking liquid rather than a fat. So if the question is about sautéing in Sherry, that might mean using Sherry as part of the cooking process, not necessarily as the primary cooking fat.

Looking at option A: It says to add sliced mushrooms to a skillet with cooking butter to cover the bottom. Then cook on medium heat, stirring so they don't stick until all the liquid is gone. So maybe the professor is thinking that "sauté in Sherry" means using Sherry as the liquid, but traditionally, you might cook in butter and then add Sherry as a deglazing agent. 

Option B suggests using Sherry as the cooking fat. That seems odd because Sherry is a liquid, not a fat. If you just use Sherry, the mushrooms might not get the necessary fat for sautéing, which could lead to sticking. Also, Sherry has alcohol and might evaporate too quickly, but the option says to stir until all the liquid is gone.

However, in some recipes, you might cook mushrooms in a mixture that includes Sherry. But the standard sauté would use butter. The professor might be right if "sauté in Sherry" is misinterpreted. question_text: How can I saute mushrooms in Sherry?

choices.A: Add sliced mushrooms to a skillet and add enough cooking butter to cover the bottom of the skillet then turn on the heat two medium and stir mushrooms so that they do not stick continues during into all of the fluid in the skillet is gone.
choices.B: Add sliced mushrooms to a skillet and add enough cooking Sherry to cover the bottom of the skillet then turn on the heat two medium and stir mushrooms so that they do not stick continues during into all of the fluid in the skillet is gone.
